name: Ensure in-use encryption keys are not scheduled for deletion
description: In-use encryption keys that are scheduled for deletion.
body:
### Overview

This control identifies cases where a KMS key is scheduled for deletion and is still linked to datastores. Deleting an active encryption key can make encrypted data unrecoverable.

Cancel deletion for keys that still protect data, or migrate the datastore to a replacement key before the deletion date.
remediationDescription:
## Remediation

Cancel deletion for keys that still protect datastores, or migrate those datastores to a replacement key before the deletion date.

### AWS

For AWS, this control identifies KMS keys that are scheduled for deletion and still linked to datastores.

1. Open the affected KMS key.
2. Review the pending deletion date and datastore dependencies.
3. If the datastore still needs the key, cancel key deletion.
4. If the key should be retired, create a replacement key and reconfigure the datastore to use it.
5. Validate access to encrypted data.
6. Re-run Cyscale sync and assessment.

CLI command:

```bash
aws kms cancel-key-deletion --key-id {{asset.idFromProvider}} --region {{asset.region}}
```

Reference: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/deleting-keys.html

The control is satisfied when no in-use datastore key is scheduled for deletion.

### GCP

1. Identify the datastore using the affected Cloud KMS key.
2. If the key version was scheduled for destruction and is still required, restore it during the restoration window.
3. If retirement is intended, create or select a replacement key version and migrate the datastore first.
4. Confirm the datastore no longer depends on the key version pending destruction.
5. Re-run Cyscale sync and assessment.

CLI example:

```bash
gcloud kms keys versions restore <key-version> --key {{asset.name}} --keyring <key-ring> --location {{asset.region}}
```

Reference: https://cloud.google.com/kms/docs/destroy-restore

The control is satisfied when the key is not scheduled for deletion while still used by a datastore.

### Azure

1. Open the affected Key Vault key.
2. If the key was deleted but is still recoverable, recover it.
3. If it is scheduled for purge, cancel the destructive path by recovering it or by migrating the datastore to a replacement key first.
4. Verify the datastore can read existing encrypted data.
5. Re-run Cyscale sync and assessment.

CLI example:

```bash
az keyvault key recover --vault-name <vault-name> --name {{asset.name}}
```

Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/soft-delete-overview

The control is satisfied when the in-use key is not scheduled for deletion.

### Alibaba

1. Open the affected Alibaba Cloud KMS key.
2. Review pending deletion and dependent datastore usage.
3. Cancel deletion if the key still protects data.
4. If retiring the key, migrate the datastore to a replacement key before deletion proceeds.
5. Validate application access to encrypted data.
6. Re-run Cyscale sync and assessment.

Reference: https://www.alibabacloud.com/help/en/kms/key-management-service/

The control is satisfied when in-use KMS keys are not scheduled for deletion.
